template.name: demo-simulated
template.description: Simulated B2B SaaS company for demos - fictional but realistic strategic depth

projects.name: My Strategy
projects.knowledgeSummary:
You're building Catalyst as an AI-powered customer success platform for mid-market B2B SaaS companies (50-500 employees, $10M-$100M ARR). Your core insight is that customer success teams are drowning in reactive churn-fighting rather than proactive value delivery. The current tools—Gainsight, ChurnZero, Totango—are essentially dashboards that surface problems after they've already happened.

Catalyst takes a fundamentally different approach: predictive intervention. By analyzing product usage patterns, support ticket sentiment, and engagement signals, you identify at-risk accounts 60-90 days before traditional health scores would flag them. But the real innovation isn't the prediction—it's the prescription. Catalyst doesn't just say 'this account is at risk,' it recommends specific playbooks based on what's worked for similar accounts.

Your ICP is crystal clear: VP of Customer Success at Series B/C SaaS companies who've just hired their 5th CSM and realized their spreadsheet-based processes won't scale. They need to prove CS ROI to their board, and you give them the data to do it. The competition is either too enterprise (Gainsight requires a dedicated admin) or too lightweight (basic health scores without actionable recommendations).

You're pursuing a land-and-expand model: start with the CS team, prove value through retention metrics, then expand into Sales (renewal forecasting) and Product (adoption analytics). Your advisory board includes two former Gainsight executives who understand where the market leader has created gaps.

suggestedQuestions.title: Daily engagement mechanism
suggestedQuestions.description: How will you prevent Catalyst from becoming just another dashboard that CS teams check once a week—what's the 'pull' mechanism that brings users back daily and makes the insights actionable in their actual workflow?

suggestedQuestions.title: Alert prioritization
suggestedQuestions.description: Your 60-90 day prediction window is compelling, but what happens when a CSM gets 50 'at-risk' alerts simultaneously? How do you help them prioritize which accounts actually need intervention vs. which are false positives?

suggestedQuestions.title: Platform expansion risk
suggestedQuestions.description: As you expand from CS into Sales and Product, how do you maintain the focused value proposition that got you initial traction—or do you risk becoming another 'platform' that tries to do everything?

messages.content: Hey! Tell me about what you're building and who it's for. What problem are you solving?

We're building Catalyst - an AI-powered customer success platform. The problem is that CS teams are completely reactive. They find out a customer is unhappy when they're already halfway out the door. By then it's too late.

Our target is mid-market B2B SaaS - companies with 50-500 employees, usually $10M-$100M ARR. They've got real CS teams (5-15 people) but they're still using spreadsheets and basic tools that just show them problems after they've happened.

The insight we have is that churn doesn't happen overnight. There are signals - drops in product usage, changes in support ticket tone, fewer logins from key stakeholders - that appear 60-90 days before traditional health scores catch them. We're building the system that catches these early and tells CSMs exactly what to do about it.

I love that insight about the 60-90 day signal window - that's a really specific and testable claim. When you talk about telling CSMs 'exactly what to do,' what does that actually look like in practice? Is it templated playbooks, or something more dynamic based on the specific situation?

It's dynamic playbooks based on pattern matching. We look at what's worked for similar accounts in similar situations.

So if we see an account where the main champion just stopped logging in, but their team usage is still strong, we look at the 500 times that pattern has occurred across our customer base. What interventions worked? Maybe 70% of the time, a 'check-in with new stakeholder' play worked. We surface that with the specific talk track and timing that worked best.

It's not just 'this account is at risk' - it's 'this account matches a pattern we've seen 500 times, here's what worked, click to execute.' The CSM still makes the call, but they have data-backed recommendations instead of gut feel.

That's powerful - you're essentially building institutional knowledge into the product. The pattern matching across 500+ similar situations is something a human CSM could never do manually.

I'm curious about your competition. Gainsight, ChurnZero, Totango are the established players. What's your take on why they haven't solved this predictive + prescriptive problem? Is it a technology gap, a market focus issue, or something else?

Gainsight is the 800-pound gorilla but they're enterprise-focused. You need a dedicated admin just to configure it. They're building for Fortune 500 CS orgs with 50+ CSMs. Their product is incredibly powerful but overwhelming for our ICP.

ChurnZero and Totango are more mid-market but they're essentially health score dashboards. They'll tell you an account is yellow or red based on rules you configure. But they don't tell you WHY or WHAT TO DO. And the rules are static - you set them up based on assumptions, not data.

The other issue is these tools are reactive by design. They're built around 'account health' as a lagging indicator. We're building around leading indicators - the signals that predict the health score before it changes.

We've got two former Gainsight execs on our advisory board. They left because they saw where the gaps were and Gainsight wasn't moving fast enough to fill them.

The advisory board angle is smart - you've got insider knowledge of exactly where the market leader is vulnerable. And the distinction between lagging vs leading indicators is a clean way to position the differentiation.

Last question: what's your go-to-market look like? Are you selling directly to VPs of CS, going through partnerships, product-led growth? And how do you think about expansion within accounts once you land?

Direct sales to VP/Head of Customer Success. These are people who just hired their 5th CSM and realized their spreadsheet processes won't scale. They need to prove CS ROI to the board - that's the burning platform.

We land with the CS team, prove value through retention and NRR metrics, then expand into adjacent use cases. Sales wants our renewal forecasting data. Product wants our adoption analytics. Finance wants our revenue impact attribution.

The wedge is CS, but we're building a customer intelligence platform. Once you've got the data infrastructure and predictions working, you can power a lot of downstream use cases.

Pricing is per-CSM seat plus a platform fee based on managed ARR. We want to align with their growth - as they scale their CS team and book, we scale with them.

themes.content: Core differentiation is catching churn signals 60-90 days before traditional health scores through leading indicators like usage patterns, support sentiment, and stakeholder engagement changes. This transforms CS from reactive churn-fighting to proactive value delivery.

Not just prediction but prescription - pattern matching across 500+ similar situations to recommend specific interventions with data-backed talk tracks and timing. The CSM still makes the call but has institutional knowledge at their fingertips.
Clear ICP: 50-500 employee SaaS companies, $10M-$100M ARR, with real CS teams (5-15 people) who've outgrown spreadsheets but find enterprise tools like Gainsight overwhelming.
Gainsight too enterprise/complex, ChurnZero/Totango are reactive dashboards. Insider advantage through former Gainsight execs on advisory board who understand where gaps exist.

Wedge through CS team, prove value via retention metrics, expand to Sales (renewal forecasting), Product (adoption analytics), Finance (revenue attribution). Building a customer intelligence platform, not just a CS tool.

strengths: Crystal clear ICP with specific firmographic criteria and burning platform
Differentiated positioning: predictive + prescriptive vs reactive dashboards
Insider competitive intelligence through advisory board
emerging: Pattern matching engine as core IP and moat
Platform expansion path beyond CS into Sales/Product/Finance
opportunities_for_enrichment: How you'll acquire enough data to make pattern matching work at launch
Technical architecture and data infrastructure requirements
Team composition and key hires needed

output.vision: To be the intelligence layer that powers every customer relationship decision, enabling B2B companies to shift from reactive churn-fighting to proactive value creation.
output.strategy: Build the definitive predictive customer success platform for mid-market B2B SaaS by combining leading indicator detection with prescriptive playbook recommendations—something the enterprise-focused incumbents are too complex to deliver and the lightweight tools are too simple to attempt.

objectives.pithy: Achieve 50 paying customers with $30K average contract value, validating product-market fit and establishing repeatable sales motion.

metric.full: Grow from 0 to 50 customers within 12 months

objectives.explanation: This objective focuses on acquiring initial paying customers to validate product-market fit and build a foundation for growth.
objectives.successCriteria: Achievement will be measured through customer count and average contract value.

objectives.pithy: Build pattern matching engine with 1000+ intervention patterns, creating the data moat that powers prescriptive recommendations.

metric.full: Grow from 0 to 1000 intervention patterns within 9 months

objectives.explanation: This objective focuses on building the core IP that differentiates Catalyst from competitors.
objectives.successCriteria: Achievement will be measured through pattern library size and recommendation accuracy.

objectives.pithy: Demonstrate measurable retention improvement for customers, proving the core value proposition of predictive intervention.

metric.full: Achieve 5% improvement in customer net retention within 6 months

objectives.explanation: This objective focuses on proving that Catalyst delivers measurable business outcomes for customers.
objectives.successCriteria: Achievement will be measured through customer retention metrics before and after implementation.

traces.claudeThoughts: Strong strategic clarity with differentiated positioning against established competitors. The predictive + prescriptive angle is compelling and defensible.

fragments.content: Predictive vs Reactive CS: Core differentiation is catching churn signals 60-90 days before traditional health scores through leading indicators like usage patterns, support sentiment, and stakeholder engagement changes.
Prescriptive Playbooks: Not just prediction but prescription - pattern matching across 500+ similar situations to recommend specific interventions with data-backed talk tracks and timing.
Mid-Market B2B SaaS Focus: Clear ICP of 50-500 employee SaaS companies, $10M-$100M ARR, with real CS teams (5-15 people) who've outgrown spreadsheets but find enterprise tools overwhelming.
Competitive Positioning: Gainsight too enterprise/complex, ChurnZero/Totango are reactive dashboards. Insider advantage through former Gainsight execs on advisory board.
Land-and-Expand Model: Wedge through CS team, prove value via retention metrics, expand to Sales (renewal forecasting), Product (adoption analytics), Finance (revenue attribution).
